pi_items.source: Confirmed in setup_task.sh line 23 and export_result.sh line 14-29. Both scripts reference this exact path for creating and copying the capacity plan report.

Confirmed as valid device type in OpenICE ICE_Device_Interface application. OpenICE documentation at https://www.openice.info/ lists device types including: 'Bernoulli, DraegerApollo, DraegerEvita4, DraegerEvitaXL, DraegerV500, FlukeProsim68, Symbiq, Ivy450C, MasimoRadical7, NellcorN595, Nonin, Capnostream20, IntellivueEthernet, IntellivueSerial, PB840, CO2_Simulator, ECG_Simulator, Pump_Simulator, IBP_Simulator, Multiparameter, NIBP_Simulator, PO_Simulator, Temp_Simulator, or BioPatch'. The simulated 'Multiparameter' monitor is used in the OpenICE demo-apps for testing and validation. Also verified in export_result.sh lines 40-46 where the script searches for device window evidence.

Verified through multiple sources: (1) Verifier.py line 105 comment states 'Check if any number is in reasonable range (1 Kbps to 5000 Kbps)'. (2) Medical device bandwidth requirements from healthcare networking research show that vital signs monitoring can operate at very low bandwidth levels. Research paper 'Network Traffic Analysis of Medical Devices' (https://arxiv.org/html/2407.13857v1) found medical devices generate modest data volumes with Bluetooth devices ranging 547-2358 bps (~0.5-2.3 Kbps). (3) HealthIT sources confirm different device types have varying minimum thresholds; simple vital signs sensors can operate below 1 Kbps. (4) ICE multiparameter monitors transmitting vital signs periodically (every 0.5-1 second) with minimal overhead could theoretically operate near 1 Kbps minimum.

Verified through multiple sources: (1) Verifier.py line 105 comment explicitly states 'reasonable (1 Kbps - 5 Mbps)' where 5 Mbps = 5000 Kbps, matching the metadata maximum exactly. (2) Medical device bandwidth requirements research shows: 12-lead ECG requires 288 kbps, 6-lead ECG 71 kbps, and wireless monitoring protocols support 20-250 Kbps. (3) For ICE multiparameter monitors with multiple vital signs channels (ECG, heart rate, respiratory rate, SpO2, temperature, blood pressure), 5000 Kbps provides reasonable upper bound for intensive monitoring scenarios. (4) Healthcare facility bandwidth recommendations (https://www.medicaldesignbriefs.com/) confirm devices support up to Mbps range. Reference sources: HealthIT.gov and TechTarget IoT healthcare network guides.

privileged_info_summary: OpenICE is an open-source medical device interoperability platform developed by the MD PnP Program at Massachusetts General Hospital (https://github.com/mdpnp/mdpnp). The task measures network bandwidth consumption of an OpenICE 'Multiparameter Monitor' simulated device, which is a valid device type supported by OpenICE's ICE_Device_Interface application (https://www.openice.info/). The task requires writing a capacity planning report to /home/ga/Desktop/capacity_plan.txt. The bandwidth thresholds are verified as realistic: minimum 1.0 Kbps aligns with low-bandwidth vital signs monitoring devices (research shows medical Bluetooth devices at ~0.5-2.3 Kbps), and maximum 5000.0 Kbps (5 Mbps) is appropriate for intensive multiparameter monitoring with multiple vital signs channels and ECG data. The data measured is synthetic in origin (from simulated devices within OpenICE), though the network traffic measurements themselves are real measurements from the Linux kernel network stack.
